questions.prompt: The impulse response of an ideal LPF is:
questions.explanation: The ideal LPF has a rectangular magnitude response. The inverse Fourier transform of a rectangle is a sinc function: h(t) = (B/π)sinc(B(t−td)/π), centred at the delay td.

questions.prompt: Why is the ideal LPF non-causal?
options: Its impulse response h(t) ≠ 0 for t < 0
Its poles are in the right half-plane
questions.correctAnswer: Its impulse response h(t) ≠ 0 for t < 0
questions.explanation: The sinc impulse response extends to t = −∞. Since h(t) is non-zero before t = 0 (or before the impulse), the system responds to inputs before they arrive — violating causality.

questions.prompt: The Fourier transform pair rect ↔ sinc means:
options: Sharp edges in frequency → sharp edges in time
Sharp edges in frequency → infinite extent in time
Sinc in frequency → rect in time
questions.correctAnswer: Sharp edges in frequency → infinite extent in time
questions.explanation: A rectangular (sharp-edged) function in frequency transforms to a sinc in time, which has infinite extent. This is a specific case of the uncertainty principle: sharp localisation in one domain → spread in the other.
Causality means the output depends only on present and past inputs, never future ones. In terms of the impulse response: h(t) must be zero for all negative time (t < 0).

questions.prompt: Can increasing the delay td make the ideal LPF causal?
options: Yes, if td is large enough
No — sinc tails always extend to t < 0
questions.correctAnswer: No — sinc tails always extend to t < 0
questions.explanation: The sinc function decays as 1/t but never reaches zero. No matter how large td is, the tails of the sinc will extend through t = 0 into negative time. The filter remains mathematically non-causal.
The normalised sinc function is sinc(x) = sin(πx)/(πx), with sinc(0) = 1. It has zeros at all non-zero integers. This is the standard definition used in signal processing.

questions.prompt: Which Fourier pair is used to derive the ideal LPF impulse response?
questions.explanation: The rectangular pulse P_W(ω) (width W) transforms to a sinc in time. For the ideal LPF with bandwidth B (total width 2B), this gives h(t) = (B/π)sinc(Bt/π), shifted by td.

questions.prompt: Are ideal HPF, BPF, and BSF also unrealisable?
options: Only the ideal LPF is unrealisable
Only LPF and HPF are unrealisable
All four ideal types are unrealisable
BSF is realisable because it has gaps
questions.correctAnswer: All four ideal types are unrealisable
questions.explanation: All four ideal filter types have rectangular (sharp-edged) magnitude responses. Each results in a sinc-type impulse response with infinite time extent → non-causal → unrealisable.

questions.prompt: The sinc function in the impulse response decays as:
questions.explanation: The sinc function sin(πx)/(πx) has an envelope that decays as 1/t. This slow decay is why the tails extend so far — they never reach zero, just get progressively smaller.

questions.prompt: What is the physical interpretation of h(t) ≠ 0 for t < 0?
options: The system has memory of past inputs
The system responds before the input arrives
questions.correctAnswer: The system responds before the input arrives
questions.explanation: If h(t) ≠ 0 for t < 0, the system produces output before the impulse input at t = 0. This means the filter 'knows' the input is coming before it arrives — a violation of causality that's physically impossible.

questions.prompt: The practical consequence of ideal filter non-realisability is:
options: Filters cannot be designed at all
Real filters must have gradual transitions between passband and stopband
questions.correctAnswer: Real filters must have gradual transitions between passband and stopband
questions.explanation: Since perfectly sharp (rectangular) frequency transitions require non-causal impulse responses, all realisable filters must have gradual transitions. This is why practical filters have transition regions between passband and stopband.

questions.prompt: The time-shift property used in deriving h(t) states:
options: Multiplying by e^{−jωtd} in frequency → shifting by td in time
Multiplying by e^{−jωtd} in frequency → scaling by td in time
Adding td to ω → shifting in time
Convolution in frequency → multiplication in time
questions.correctAnswer: Multiplying by e^{−jωtd} in frequency → shifting by td in time
questions.explanation: The Fourier time-shift property: if F{f(t)} = F(ω), then F{f(t−td)} = F(ω)·e^{−jωtd}. The exponential factor in frequency corresponds to a time delay of td in the time domain.
